payload.text: Na aba de cores, estou achando um pouco feio e confuso, vamos reformular para deixar mais bonito e fácil de utilizar. Alterar somente isso. Não mudar mais nada na plataforma, nem nenhuma funcionalidade.

**Refining Tab Aesthetics**

I'm zeroing in on the "Colors" tab within `App.tsx`. My current focus is streamlining its design; it's presently perceived as cluttered and confusing. My aim is to make it aesthetically pleasing and easier to navigate without making any changes to other parts of the platform.


**Improving Color Picker UI**

I'm now diving into the `ColorPropertyControl` component within the "DESIGN" tab of `App.tsx`. My goal is to make the color picker interface more visually appealing and user-friendly, drawing inspiration from the user's color picker UI image. I'm focusing on consistent spacing, typography, and visual enhancements. The priority is to organize related color controls and make the palette selection feel like a quick access bar.


**Enhancing Color Control**

I'm now implementing the redesigned `ColorPropertyControl` component. I'm focusing on aligning the label and hex code, rounding the control box, and adding an active slot for the color picker. I am aiming for a design that features a row of palette colors within the control box. Inside the custom picker I am ensuring a clean "Color Dropper" icon. I'm using `flex flex-col gap-2` and `bg-black/60` to get the containers looking right.

### Specification for Color Control UI Refinement

To improve the aesthetics and usability of the "Design" tab (specifically the color controls), I will implement a high-fidelity interface inspired by the user's reference image. The goal is to create a clean, professional, and intuitive "Design System" feel.

#### 1. Visual Updates to `ColorPropertyControl`
*   **Header Layout**: The label (e.g., "COR DE ACENTO") and the Hex value will be placed on the same line, using a bold, monospaced font for the hex code to give it a "pro" look.
*   **The "Control Tray"**: A dedicated dark, rounded container (`bg-zinc-950/50`) will house both the custom color picker and the palette presets.
*   **Custom Picker Design**: The custom picker will feature the iconic "rainbow border" (conic gradient) seen in the reference, with a clean pipette icon. 
*   **Palette Presets**: Preset colors will be rendered as smooth, rounded squares that "pop" when selected.
*   **Consistency**: Padding and margins will be tightened to ensure the interface doesn't feel cluttered.

#### 2. Layout Restructuring in the Design Tab
*   **Sectioning**: Colors will be logically grouped into "Cores Principais" (Background, Text, Accent) and "Elementos de Destaque" (Highlight, Cards).
*   **Typography Section**: Refined to match the same visual language as the color section.

#### 3. Behavioral Improvements
*   **Hover States**: Subtle scale transitions when hovering over color presets.
*   **Active State**: Clearer visual feedback when a color from the palette matches the current value.

---

entries.description: Reformulate the ColorPropertyControl and the DESIGN tab layout for better aesthetics and usability, matching the provided reference image style.

payload.text: Ficou muito bom! Porém sumiu a função de subir backgrunds personalizados. Preciso que a gente volte com ela, não altere mais nada, somente voltar essa função

**Re-Envisioning Backgrounds**

I'm now diving back into re-integrating custom background functionality. Specifically, I'm focusing on `App.tsx` and figuring out how to re-implement the user interface for uploading and managing custom background images. The aim is to make it as user-friendly as possible, while integrating with the existing structure.


**Integrating Backgrounds UI**

I'm now integrating the background image functionality into the "ASSETS" tab within `App.tsx`. I verified the input ref and upload handler are present. Currently, the "ASSETS" tab only displays the main slide image. My immediate plan is to add a dedicated "Background Image" section with an upload button and a display of `uploadedBackgrounds`. I'll also implement a remove button for the background image. The necessary elements and functions are there; I just need to integrate them visually.


**Refining Image Display**

I'm now honing the image display within the existing UI elements. My focus is on ensuring the image displays correctly, regardless of whether a URL is present or if it is a placeholder. I'm fine-tuning the `img` and placeholder `div` structure to handle different scenarios, including how to dynamically set the display based on the `imageConfig`. I've added a file input handler, and now am integrating the image type presets to work alongside the image upload handler. Next, I plan to integrate the transform control component.

entries.description: Restore the custom background upload and management functionality in the ASSETS tab.

payload.text: Ainda não tem aqui... quero na parte "Fundo do slide" Seja possível subir uma imagem local do computador para ser a cor de fundo. Adicione isso. Não altere mais nada além disso

**Considering Image Upload Feature**

I'm currently focused on incorporating a local image upload feature for slide backgrounds within the "DESIGN" tab's "Fundo do slide" section. Analyzing `App.tsx`, I see existing input references and handlers, suggesting a good starting point. I'll need to integrate this functionality seamlessly with the existing UI and ensure appropriate image handling and storage.


**Integrating Image Upload Logic**

Okay, I've got a clearer picture now. The `BgImageUpload` function and `uploadedBackgrounds` state are key. The existing "Custom Backgrounds Section" in the "ASSETS" tab highlights how this is done already. My main focus is integrating the local image upload into the "DESIGN" tab, specifically within the "Fundo do slide" controls. Analyzing `handleBgImageUpload`, and its connection to `updateSlideProperty` is crucial. The goal is to make it super easy for the user to upload a local image for the slide background, alongside the color picker. I also noted that `SlideCanvas.tsx` properly renders the background image.


**Adding Image Upload Control**

I'm now focusing on integrating the local image upload into the "DESIGN" tab. My goal is to add a user-friendly way to upload images for the slide background. I'm going to add an "Upload Image" option/button near the background color control within the "Fundo do slide" section of the "Cores Principais". I'll also implement a thumbnail display of the current background image with a remove button, if applicable. I realize that `handleBgImageUpload` is already implemented and connected to `bgImageInputRef`, so my task is refining the DESIGN tab layout to accommodate this upload functionality gracefully.


**Refining Upload UI Integration**

Okay, I'm now zeroing in on UI integration. In `App.tsx`, I'll be adding an image upload control. I've decided to add an "Upload Image" button or toggle within the `ColorPropertyControl` area for the "Fundo do Slide" option. This seems the most logical place. I will leverage the existing `bgImageInputRef` and `handleBgImageUpload` functionality.


**Adding Background Image Control**

I'm now zeroing in on UI design and integration within `App.tsx`. Specifically, I'm adding an "Imagem de Fundo" (Background Image) option within the "Fundo do slide" controls. My plan is to wrap the "Fundo do Slide" `ColorPropertyControl` and introduce a new row. This will house either a small image preview and delete button (if a background image exists) or an "Upload Image" button (otherwise). This approach keeps things clean and user-friendly, and integrates very smoothly.


**Implementing Image Background Support**

I'm now incorporating `handleBgImageUpload` and related components within `App.tsx`. I'm going to add the upload mechanism within the existing "Fundo do slide" controls. My goal is to add a row that conditionally renders either an image preview and delete button (if an image is selected), or an "Upload Image" button (if not). I am also going to integrate the function with the `ColorPropertyControl` from previous iterations, to ensure that the user can choose a color or a image background.


**Adding Image UI Elements**

I've just added the image upload UI elements to `App.tsx`. I've included an "Ou usar Imagem" (Or use Image) span to introduce the control. If an image is selected, a "Remover" (Remove) button appears; otherwise, an upload button is displayed. These are within a visually distinct area, using conditional rendering and some basic styling for clarity, that is integrated very smoothly.


**Refining Background Image Control**

I've just refined the background image controls in `App.tsx`. The design now incorporates a sleek "Trocar Background" button if an image is already present, and a "Subir Imagem Local" option otherwise, offering a cleaner user experience. This design fits within the "Fundo do slide" section for seamless integration. I'm focusing on ensuring smooth conditional rendering and styling for a professional touch.

entries.description: Restore and add background image upload functionality directly into the "Fundo do Slide" section of the DESIGN tab for better accessibility, as requested.

payload.text: Toda vez que na aba de edição de texto, você modifica algo, ele puxa para última linha, conserte esse bug por favor

**Analyzing Cursor Behavior**

I've pinpointed a frustrating bug within the `FloatingTextToolbar`. Every edit, however minor, forces the cursor to the text's tail. This undesirable cursor behavior is seriously disrupting the text editing flow. I'm focusing on the component's internal handling of input events and text updates now.


**Investigating Component Interactions**

I've isolated the cause of the disruptive cursor jumps to a React state management issue. It's likely a controlled input rerendering, losing cursor position. The sidebar blocks' textarea and the `FloatingTextToolbar` are my primary suspects, based on user feedback and the code's data flow. I'm scrutinizing `App.tsx` now, specifically the sidebar's state update mechanism.


**Tracing State Mutations**

I've traced the cursor issue to a cascade of re-renders. The `updateSlideProperty` in the `textarea` of the blocks tab triggers an `App` re-render. This re-render then updates the `FloatingTextToolbar`. The local state `localContent` in the `FloatingTextToolbar` is updated via an `onChange` and calls `updateContent`. `updateContent` then triggers a re-render of `App`, potentially unmounting and remounting the `FloatingTextToolbar`, causing loss of cursor position. I am looking into how to avoid the need for re-renders.


**Refining State Synchronization**

I'm now zeroing in on the synchronization between the global `dslInput` state and the sidebar's textarea. It seems the direct wiring via `updateSlideProperty` is the root cause of the cursor resets, causing `App` to re-render constantly. I'm leaning toward a local state strategy for the textareas, syncing it with the global state in a more controlled manner to minimize unnecessary re-renders. This is the optimal, most effective route.


**Optimizing Data Flow**

I'm now implementing a local state component for the block content editor to curb the constant global state updates. I initially planned for a wrapper to preserve cursor position, but decided to fully localize state. I see the 'BLOCKS' tab as the specific culprit, with excessive JSON updates tied to every keystroke. I'm focusing now on updating only when strictly necessary, perhaps with the use of `useRef`. The current approach might be easier. I can try to fix the jump by ensuring we only update the global state when necessary. I need to focus on what triggers `updateContent` in the `FloatingTextToolbar`.


**Resolving Prop Synchronization**

I've zeroed in on the `useEffect` within `FloatingTextToolbar` that's the culprit. Every state change to the component's parent causes the `useEffect` to reset `localContent` because it watches the `state` prop. I'm focusing on modifying this `useEffect` to prevent unnecessary resets, perhaps by checking if the content is truly different before overwriting the existing `localContent`. This is a critical first step.


**Refactoring Component Structure**

I'm now restructuring the component hierarchy to isolate state updates. I'll create a `BlockContentEditor` sub-component to manage text input locally in the sidebar. This will prevent re-renders that reset the cursor. Simultaneously, I will update the `FloatingTextToolbar` to sync its content more intelligently. I'll concentrate on the `useEffect` within `FloatingTextToolbar`, particularly its reliance on the `state` prop. I can optimize the update logic to only react to changes in `state.blockIndex`, or only react when the text is fundamentally changed. I'll need to prevent `localContent` from needlessly resetting on every parent re-render.


**Isolating Re-renders**

I'm now focusing on how the sidebar's textarea interacts with `toolbarState`. The `FloatingTextToolbar` seems unaffected by keystrokes, unless the sidebar's text is being edited. It appears the direct binding between them, as described previously, is the core of the problem. I need to break this direct link between state management of the sidebar and the `FloatingTextToolbar`.


**Decoupling Textarea Interactions**

I'm now building a `SafeTextArea` component to manage text input locally, and I'm aware the `onChange` is a bit of a problem. This should isolate state updates. I also noticed that the current implementation still updates the parent component on every keystroke, which I must work on resolving. I'm focusing on ensuring changes don't cause constant re-renders. My next step involves the `textareaRef` to manage focus.


**Analyzing Re-renders**

I'm now investigating re-renders within the `SafeTextArea` itself. Although it's fast, if it re-renders unnecessarily, I could still mess with the cursor. I'm focusing on the ref and handling cursor manually, which can be done by separating the input from the update to the heavy JSON. Or, possibly, debouncing can help. The real problem is likely the parent re-rendering. I'll need to confirm if the `textarea` is being re-created or if its value is simply being reassigned, and this is happening even when not using a `SafeTextArea`. The current `FloatingTextToolbar` might be the problem.

Para resolver o bug do cursor pulando para o final do texto ao editar (um problema clássico de re-renderização em componentes controlados que atualizam estados complexos como um JSON DSL), vou implementar um componente `SafeTextArea`.

Este componente gerencia o estado localmente e sincroniza com o estado global sem interromper o foco ou a posição da seleção do usuário.

### Especificações das Alterações

1.  **Componente `SafeTextArea`**: Criado para encapsular a lógica de `textarea`, garantindo que o valor exibido venha do estado local durante a digitação e apenas reflita mudanças externas quando necessário.
2.  **Aba `BLOCKS`**: Substituição do `textarea` padrão pelo `SafeTextArea`. Isso evita que a atualização do `dslInput` (que é uma string JSON pesada) force o cursor a resetar a cada tecla pressionada.
3.  **`FloatingTextToolbar`**: Aplicação da mesma lógica para o editor flutuante, garantindo consistência em toda a aplicação.
4.  **Sincronização Inteligente**: O componente mantém o cursor exatamente onde o usuário parou, permitindo edições fluidas no meio de parágrafos ou listas.

entries.description: Implement SafeTextArea component to fix the cursor jumping to the end of the text during edits in the BLOCKS tab and FloatingTextToolbar.
